ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba or broth-free Japanese noodle dishes with cheese and cod roe. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles) is the closest available category. No specific mazesoba or Japanese broth-free noodle code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for mazesoba or this specific dish variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of mazesoba at a Japanese ramen restaurant, approximately 450 g, comprising thick wheat noodles, mentaiko cod roe, grilled melted cheese, tare sauce, and toppings.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: cooked wheat noodles (SR Legacy #20121), spiced fish roe / mentaiko (USDA FDC #174196 analogous), cheddar cheese grilled (USDA FDC #173414), sesame oil, soy sauce, and egg yolk; weighted by typical mazesoba recipe proportions.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy and Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for Grilled Cheese with Cod Roe Mazesoba exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FDC, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 55% cooked thick wheat noodles, 15% mentaiko (spiced pollock roe), 12% grilled cheddar cheese, 8% egg yolk, 5% soy-based tare sauce, 3% sesame oil, and 2% garnish (nori, green onion). Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of wheat noodles and pasta — analogous estimate based on 'Noodles, instant, boiled' and 'Spaghetti, white, boiled' GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; adjusted downward for protein and fat co-ingestion from cheese and cod roe.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mazesoba. GI of 47 is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat noodles (GI range 35–55, mean ~47 for thick Asian wheat noodles). The substantial protein from mentaiko and fat from cheese are expected to lower postprandial glucose response relative to plain noodles. GL per serving = (47 × 23.5 × 450 / 100) / 100 = approximately 49.7 g. This is a high glycemic load per serving due to large portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based tare sauce, mentaiko, and cheese
hypertension.guidance: A single bowl of this dish may deliver approximately 2,340 mg sodium (520 mg per 100 g × 450 g serving), exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium tare.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving despite moderate GI, driven by large noodle portion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of thick wheat noodles is moderate-to-low (~47), the large carbohydrate load per bowl (~106 g per serving) produces a high glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion or substitute with lower-carbohydrate noodle alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and egg yolk, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (~14 g per serving from grilled cheese and egg yolk) and high sodium (~2,340 mg per serving) is unfavorable for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose smaller portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled Cheese with Cod Roe Mazesoba is a Japanese broth-free noodle dish (mazesoba) served at specialty ramen restaurants in Singapore and Japan. Unlike conventional ramen, mazesoba contains no soup broth; instead, thick wheat noodles are coated in a rich soy-based tare sauce and sesame oil, then topped with spiced pollock roe (mentaiko), a grilled melted cheese layer, raw or soft-cooked egg yolk, nori, and green onions. The diner mixes all components together before eating, creating a creamy, umami-rich, slightly spicy coating on the noodles. The texture is chewy from the thick noodles, creamy from the melted cheese and egg yolk, and briny-spicy from the mentaiko. Nutritionally, this dish is energy-dense (~890 kcal per bowl), high in sodium (~2,340 mg per serving), and provides meaningful protein (~41 g per serving) from the cod roe, cheese, and egg. The glycemic index is moderate-to-low (~47) for the noodle base, but the glycemic load per serving is high due to the large noodle portion. Clinically, the dish is of concern for hypertension due to very high sodium, and for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from cheese and egg yolk. It is not suitable for halal diets due to the use of pork-derived ingredients in some restaurant preparations, and contains major allergens including gluten, dairy, egg, fish, sesame, and soy.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
How much sodium is in mazesoba?
Where can I find mazesoba in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for Grilled Cheese with Cod Roe Mazesoba exists in any approved primary nutrient database (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FDC, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FDC SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported across the component sources used for this estimated dish; individual ingredients such as egg yolk contain small amounts but the composite value is not defensible without direct measurement. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA FDC component data and carry additional uncertainty. The input record described the food group as 'Dairy and eggs'; this has been corrected to 'Grains and staples' as the primary caloric and volumetric component is wheat noodles. The input description 'Soup comes with all Mazesoba' appears to be a menu note indicating that a small side soup is served alongside the mazesoba bowl, which is common practice at mazesoba restaurants; the dish itself is broth-free. Serving size of 450 g is estimated from typical restaurant portion sizes for mazesoba in Singapore. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_g / 100) / 100 = (47 × 23.5 × 450 / 100) / 100 = 49.7.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is a precise canonical English name. Japanese local name provided as required by G6 for Japanese cuisine. Romanized form also present.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: All four core macros (energy_kcal 198.0, protein_g 9.2, fat_g 7.8, carbohydrate_g 23.5) are present, numeric, and non-zero. Nutrient source is USDA FDC SR Legacy with explicit component weighting documented.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.0), sodium_mg (520.0), saturated_fat_g (3.1), and sugar_g (1.2) are all present as numeric values and are plausible for this dish type.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (47, numeric > 0), gi_category (low), glycemic_load_per_serving (49.7), and gi_source are all present. GI estimated by analogy from University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat noodles, with estimation basis documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) used as closest_parent with gap note. FoodEx2 A00LC (Pasta and noodles) used as closest_parent with gap note. SNOMED CT is none with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, explains what the food is, preparation method, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (high concern, sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, glycemic load), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate concern, saturated fat and sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.

All strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
